🔷 Now Hiring: Technical Program Manager – Data Modernization
📍 New York City, NY (Hybrid) | 📄 Contract Opportunity (Extension Possible)
TechDigital Corporation is partnering with Indium Softwares to hire an experienced Technical Program Manager to lead a large-scale Customer Support Data Modernization Program. This is a high-impact role focused on building a unified, scalable, and AI-ready data platform.
🔹 Role Overview
We are seeking a seasoned Technical Program Manager with strong experience in enterprise data transformation initiatives. The ideal candidate will drive execution from strategy to delivery, ensuring alignment across engineering, data, and analytics teams.
🔹 Key Responsibilities
Lead a multi-phase data modernization program from planning to execution
Translate strategic objectives into a structured roadmap with clear milestones and dependencies
Drive cross-functional collaboration across engineering, data, and analytics teams
Establish and manage program governance, reporting cadence, and stakeholder communication
Identify and mitigate risks, dependencies, and blockers proactively
Ensure successful delivery of a scalable, future-ready data platform
🔹 Required Experience
12+ years of overall experience with at least 4+ years in Technical Program Management
Proven experience leading large-scale data transformation/modernization programs
Strong understanding of:
Data Management
Data Warehousing
Data Lakes
Data Modeling practices
Data migration and transformation strategies
🔹 Preferred Skills
Exposure to SQL or data querying concepts
Strong analytical and problem-solving skills
Excellent communication and stakeholder management abilities
🔹 Why Join
Lead a strategic, enterprise-wide transformation initiative
Work with cross-functional teams on cutting-edge data platforms
Opportunity to drive AI-ready data architecture and innovation
📩 Interested candidates are encouraged to apply or connect directly:
Rajkumar Marjing
IT Recruiter | TechDigital Corporation
📧 rajkumar@techdigitalcorp.com

Every face in this image is fake.

No model was paid.
No studio was booked.
No photographer showed up.
No one signed a contract.

This is 100% AI. And it's already being used to sell products, build brands, and run ads — right now.

I just got out of 2 meetings today and them their new UGC creators, both of them thought I had done a photoshoot and found actual people but no even close.

Here's what that means for your business:

That R80,000 photoshoot? Gone.
That R30,000 UGC creator video? Gone.
That R45,000/month agency retainer? Gone.

AI generates hyper-realistic models, products, scenes and video ads in minutes.
AI runs your Meta Ads and Google Ads 24/7 — optimising every rand while you sleep.
AI writes the copy, designs the creative, posts the content, and reads the data.

And it does it for a fraction of what you're currently paying.

━━━

Your competitor is not sitting in a photo studio anymore.

They're prompting AI to generate 20 ad variants before lunch.
They're letting AI kill the creatives that waste money and double the ones that print it.
They're building content machines — not hiring content teams.

While you're waiting for a photographer's calendar to open up...
They're already live.
Already testing.
Already winning.

━━━

I want you to ask yourself one honest question:

If AI can produce a face so realistic you can't tell it's fake — what else is it capable of doing for your marketing?

The answer is: everything you've been paying humans to do.

Every post.
Every ad.
Every video.
Every lead.

This isn't a threat to creativity.
It's the end of the excuse that great marketing is expensive.

━━━

Comment "AI" below and I'll send you the exact toolkit that makes this possible for your business — in 7 days or less.

This is a high-visibility role blending strategic oversight with hands-on technical governance.
📍 Key Details:
Role: Program Manager Consultant
Location: NYC (Onsite)
Duration: 6 Months (Contract)
Focus: Cross-domain technology (Cloud, Data & AI, Infrastructure, Cyber)
🛠️ What You’ll Do:
Executive Visibility: Develop high-quality Power BI dashboards and scorecards for leadership (CTO/DCTO levels).
Strategic Alignment: Ensure tech solutions align with enterprise architecture and business objectives.
Resource Mastery: Track employee bandwidth, resource allocation, and project health metrics.
Cross-Functional Leadership: Coordinate across Cloud, Data, Network, and Cybersecurity teams.
✅ Must-Have Credentials:
Certifications: PMP, CSM, and Agile (Required). ITIL Foundation (Preferred).

#Hiring
Title: Agentic AI Developer (Python) — Vertex AI RAG + Graph/Vector   Datastores
Location: Berkeley Heights, NJ (5 days onsite)
Visa: All (Except OPT/CPT)
Tax Term: Contract

Role summary
We’re looking for a strong agentic AI developer who can build and productionize Vertex AI–based RAG systems (Vertex AI Search / Vertex AI RAG patterns), design reliable tool-using agents, and work comfortably with vector databases and graph databases. You’ll own end-to-end delivery: ingestion → retrieval → agent orchestration → evaluation → deployment.
What you’ll do
Design and implement RAG pipelines on Google Cloud / Vertex AI (chunking, embeddings, indexing, retrieval, reranking, grounding).
Build agentic workflows (tool use, planning, reflection/guardrails, structured outputs) using Python-first frameworks.
Integrate agents with Graph DBs (e.g., Neo4j, JanusGraph, Neptune) and Vector DBs (e.g., Vertex Vector Search, Pinecone, Weaviate, Milvus, pgvector).
Create robust data ingestion/ETL from PDFs, docs, webpages, and internal sources; implement metadata strategy and access control.
Define and run evaluation (retrieval metrics, answer quality, hallucination/grounding checks), and improve system quality iteratively.
Ship to production: APIs, monitoring/observability, cost/performance optimization, CI/CD, and security best practices.
Must-have skills
Strong Python (clean architecture, async, testing, typing, packaging).
Proven experience building RAG solutions (hybrid search, reranking, chunking strategies, embeddings, prompt + schema design).
Hands-on with Vertex AI and GCP fundamentals (IAM, logging/monitoring, Cloud Run/GKE, storage).
Experience with at least one agentic framework (e.g., LangGraph/LangChain, LlamaIndex, Semantic Kernel, AutoGen) and tool/function calling patterns.
Solid knowledge of vector search concepts and at least one vector DB in production.
Comfortable with graph data modeling and graph querying (Cypher/Gremlin/SPARQL basics).
Strong engineering practices: code reviews, testing, telemetry, secure-by-design, reliability mindset.
Nice-to-have
Knowledge graphs for RAG (entity linking, graph traversal + retrieval fusion).
Streaming/messaging (Pub/Sub, Kafka), document pipelines (Document AI), and multilingual retrieval.
Experience with evaluation tooling (RAGAS, TruLens, custom eval harnesses), prompt/version management.
Frontend integration (basic React/Next.js) or platform enablement (internal developer tooling).
 
Send resumes to sam@ojusllc.com for immediate response. 

#linkedinfamily #itjobs #agenticai #MLlearning #techjobs #usitjobs
#onsite #C2C #W2

Most professionals with 10+ years of domain expertise have no idea they're sitting on a $150/hour contract.

AI labs can't train financial models without people who actually understand bond pricing. They can't build clinical AI without MDs who've run real patient cases. They can't evaluate silicon design without engineers who've taped out real chips.

The credential you spent a decade or more earning is exactly what they're buying right now.

Here's what's moving this week in the AI contract market:

✅ Fixed Income Trading Pro — $150–180/hr | US 
Bond pricing, yield curves, trade execution logic. Teaching AI how markets actually move.

✅ Tax Research Expert — $100–180/hr | US 
Federal and state domain depth. Former IRS or tax attorney background hits hardest here.

✅ RTL Design Engineer — $100–175/hr | US, CA 
SystemVerilog, ASIC flows, 3–10 years in. One of the quieter high-demand niches in AI eval.

✅ Primary Care Physician — $100–130/hr | US 
Board-certified MDs annotating clinical AI outputs. Your diagnostic experience is the product.

The pattern is consistent: AI labs are not hiring generalists for these roles. They are hiring the person who went deep in one field and stayed there.

What's the most niche credential or domain you have that you've never thought to put a price tag on?

This week's full list: https://lnkd.in/eZq2kCtJ

🌪️ Knowledge that isn't institutionalized is quite literally a ticking time bomb.

Relying on the individual discretion of a "star" employee for business continuity is accepting a high-stakes operational gamble 🧨. Giants like Toyota and Amazon don't dominate their industries by hiring isolated geniuses; they do it by designing an #optimization where the system is the protagonist. In the harsh reality of #payroll, losing a key player without defined processes triggers months of paralysis and a massive drain of critical intelligence 📉.

The #leadership at companies like McDonald’s and Starbucks understood decades ago that global consistency is born from process engineering, not luck. This isn't about dehumanizing work—it’s about ensuring that #AI and technology are built on solid foundations rather than hallway whims. A business that depends on "stars" to function is, in essence, an administrative prison that stifles any real vision for the future 🏢.

The architecture implemented by FedEx allows the #operation to flow regardless of who is in charge of the shift, eliminating any operational #bottleneck caused by absence. If your current structure doesn't allow for this rotation without a drop in quality, your assets are being held hostage by the human factor. It is time to transform "memory-based talent" into documented assets that belong to the brand, not to an individual’s contract 📊.

If your most important employee didn't show up tomorrow, what percentage of your operation would collapse before noon? If that answer causes you anxiety, it’s time to professionalize your systems so they work for you, and not the other way around. Which critical process in your organization currently relies entirely on a single person's memory?

She helped me see that the complexity I'd navigated in enterprise systems, competing stakeholder priorities, and designing under real constraints was exactly the kind of experience the market needs right now. She genuinely cares about her clients. Alex held me accountable in a way that actually worked: daily standups, consistent check-ins, honest pushback when I was letting perfectionism get in the way of progress. She stayed invested in my progress well beyond our sessions.

I walked away with a portfolio and narrative I'm genuinely proud of, and work I'm excited about.

If you're stuck or going through a real career transition, not just a job search but a full reassessment of where you've been and where you're going, Alex is the person I'd call.

Thank you Alexandra Hurworth (PCC)!

"The End of the World As We Know It — And I Feel Fine"

Post 1
The Timeline Nobody Is Posting

Humanoid robots are on factory floors at BMW, Mercedes-Benz, and Amazon. Today. Not in a lab. Not in a pilot. On the floor.

AI agents are closing books, reviewing contracts, handling tier-1 support, and screening candidates. Not perfectly. Well enough to stop hiring for those roles.

This is not a 2030 story. This is a 2025 status report.

Here's the timeline nobody in your portfolio review meeting is saying out loud:

2025. The beachhead year.
Tier 1 manufacturing is already automated. AI handles the first layer of every knowledge work function. Headcount freezes begin. Most companies call it something else.

2026. The middle market wakes up.
PE sponsors mandate automation roadmaps. First wave of white-collar displacement hits. Paralegals. Junior analysts. AR/AP teams. Not eliminated — compressed. CFOs who built the evaluation framework are running it. Everyone else is buying vendor decks.

2027. The compounding problem.
Robot unit economics cross the threshold. $80K–$100K per unit. Three-year payback. Default assumption in any labor-intensive business. The labor market bifurcates. High-skill human judgment commands a premium. Everything else is under pressure.

2028. The data reckoning.
Companies that invested in clean data infrastructure are deploying AI at scale. Companies that didn't are stuck. The 18–24 month readiness gap shows up on the P&L. Revenue per employee diverges sharply between the prepared and the frozen.

2029. The exit narrative changes permanently.

PE buyers underwrite human capital differently. High labor dependency with no automation roadmap gets a haircut on multiple. EBITDA adjustments include automation displacement risk as standard diligence. The CFOs who built this framework early are in the room when the LOI gets signed.

This is not speculation. Every data point on this timeline is already visible in public markets, venture deployment, and Fortune 500 workforce decisions.

The only question is whether you're building the framework now or explaining later why you didn't.

Next: What every year on this timeline means for your portfolio — and the capital decisions attached to each one.

Brad Wolfe | wolfepacks.com

Why your 2026 AI budget won’t fix a broken process.

Lately, I’ve seen a trend: leaders planning 2026 budgets thinking automation is the cure-all for operational headaches. But here’s the thing, automation is just an accelerator. If you automate a mess, you just get a faster mess.

In most organizations, complexity, not cost, is the real bottleneck. We layer on steps and "just in case" protocols until the actual work is buried. Then we expect AI to dig us out. 

Before signing that SaaS contract or hiring an automation specialist, try a radical step: Reduce. 

At Solved., we help you ask the hard questions: Do we actually need this step? Who does it benefit? Is there a simpler way before we go digital? 

Simplifying first doesn’t just save money; it makes future automation ten times more effective. It clears the path so your team and your tech can actually perform.

If your systems feel a bit off and you want actionable progress, not just more reports, I’d love to chat. We get in the trenches with you to streamline things first.

Lawyers aren't being replaced by AI. The ones ignoring it are being replaced by lawyers who use it.

>> HARVEY AI
Harvey is now being used inside some of the biggest law firms in the world , Allen & Overy, PwC Legal, and others. Associates are using it to draft contracts, research case law, and summarize hundreds of pages of documents in minutes. Work that used to take a junior lawyer 6 hours is getting done before lunch. The firms aren't hiring fewer lawyers. They're billing more hours because each lawyer can take on more.

>> CONTRACT REVIEW
Startups and in-house legal teams are using tools like Ironclad and Spellbook to review contracts in real time inside Microsoft Word. A legal ops manager at a mid-size company can now flag risky clauses, compare terms against a playbook, and redline a 40-page vendor agreement without waiting days for outside counsel. One task that used to cost $800 in billable hours is now a 12-minute job.

>> SOLO PRACTITIONERS
This is the part nobody talks about. Solo lawyers and small firms are using AI to compete with firms 10 times their size. A family law attorney in Ohio can now generate a demand letter, research local precedents, and prep for a deposition using tools that cost less per month than a single hour of paralegal time. The playing field is shifting fast.

>> LITIGATION SUPPORT
Paralegals and litigation support teams are using AI to process discovery documents. What used to mean weeks of reviewing thousands of emails and files is now sorted, tagged, and summarized in a fraction of the time. One legal tech team reported cutting document review time by over 70 percent on a recent case.

The lawyers losing work right now are not the ones using AI. They are the ones billing clients for tasks AI already does in seconds.

Are you in legal, or work with legal teams? What are you seeing on the ground?

The 15 min pre podcast call that turned into an episode.

I connected with Louis Fernandes. He runs Edge Partners AI. They help commercial real estate companies implement AI systems when their operations are growing faster than their processes can keep up.

His sweet spot: businesses doing a million plus a year with 3 to 15 employees. Growing, but their systems are breaking.
Sound familiar?

When I asked him what drives him, he said something I won't easily forget.
His wife told him: "You just help people."
And he said, "Yeah. That is my purpose. I can live with that."

That resonated with me because that is the same reason why I wanted to be an entrepreneur. I want to use my talents and gifts to serve people in a way that only I can.

And this is also the reason I am going all in on AI.

Not because the technology is cool. Because it helps me buy back my time to do the work that I started my businesses to do.

Before AI, I was cycling through virtual assistants and admins. Every time one left, I was back to doing everything. Logging deals. Reviewing title reports. Sending follow-ups. Filing contracts. All of it falling on me.

So before I hired my next admin, I decided to see what AI could handle first.
What I've learned and built has changed the way I will run businesses forever.
The more I gave to AI, the more time and freedom I got back. I got to be on the phones with sellers, which is where I belong. I got to build relationships, which is what I am actually good at.

Here is my philosophy:
The real advantage of artificial intelligence is that it gives us the opportunity to be authentic individuals.

For me it's Salsa dancing on weeknights, faith, and improving my fitness. For you it can be working on your golf swing and spending time with your family.

We can stop being buried under work that was never the thing that inspired us to go into business in the first place.

Two things any business owner can take from this:
1. Before you hire your next employee, audit what AI could handle first. The role you are hiring for might be 5 tasks that should be automated and 2 that deserve a higher-level hire.

2. Find the people already doing what you want to do. A 15-minute conversation with the right person can redirect your entire year. This one did for me.

Thank you Louis Fernandes. Looking forward to The AI Edge podcast soon.

♻️ Repost if this resonates.

By the way, I built a free diagnostic that scores how ready your business is to operate with AI. Takes 3 minutes.

We have a growing number of AI leaders, founders, and integration experts… Some have seen the way to jump to IT from other occupations... The opportunity is ripe because leaders aren't putting two and two together. There are leaders in AI that are bringing contracts and many are still following the same path:

-Build on top of LLMs
-Wrap APIs around them
-Call it transformation

If you’re technical, you’ve probably already seen the cracks:
-Inconsistent outputs
-Heavy reliance on prompts and tuning
-Latency between decision and execution
-Governance applied after the fact
-Cost of tokens to support the technology

LLMs are powerful but they’re not a foundation. They’re a tool that should only be used for research and development only.

The real question is:
-What are we actually building our systems on?

Because if every pitch sounds the same… and every architecture depends on probabilistic outputs at critical points that are documented in the news, on posts and even social media… Then we’re not innovating, we’re repeating.

The next phase of AI won’t be won by better prompts or bigger models.
It will be won by:
-structured execution
-governed systems
-architectures that decide when AI should act—not just how it responds

If you’re hiring or ready to sign a contract, it’s worth asking:
Are you getting a strategy… or just a more polished version of what’s already struggling? Different thinking isn’t optional anymore—it’s necessary.

The VP of Martech at one of the top data companies doesn't even open greenhouse anymore because all the damn resumes and cover letters look identical. 

Everyone is taking the JD + resume and asking an LLM to spit out a tailored application. There's nothing wrong with doing that, but literally everyone else is... so it's not the hack you thought it was. 

Standing out as a candidate when you apply for job right now has never been harder. 

"Job hunting is a game of quality, not quantity" has never been more true. While everyone else is using AI tools to auto-apply for 200 jobs every day, take the human approach. Get a warm intro. Craft a cover letter yourself. Build a unique portfolio of your work. Send a thank you note. These are all super basic job hunt tactics but they seem to be a dying art. 

Today is part 2/3 of our Martech Job Hunt Survival Guide at Humans of Martech. Last week Darrell Alfonso shared his layoff + job hunt rollercoaster last year and we talked about tapping into a hidden job market. 

This guide is for people that are #opentowork or are stuck in a dead-end gig and want something better. Heck, this guide is useful to you even if you love your job, because you never know when layoffs could hit. 

Today we focus on how to stand out from a sea of sloppy AI applicants:
• Why Most Hiring Managers Can’t Actually Evaluate Your AI Experience
• How to Build a Marketing Ops Portfolio When Your Work Is Buried in Tools
• Why Contract Work Is a Strategic Move Job Seekers Right Now
• Which Job Search Tools Help and Which Ones Waste Your Time
• Why Creating LinkedIn Content Can Work Even When Nobody Is Watching

Still buzzing after speaking on the main stage at Codemotion Madrid 2026🚀about the new Web UI features landing on the UI platform ✨

Beyond my talk, I had the chance to attend several inspiring sessions. A couple of highlights that really stuck with me:

🤖One was Andrey Breslav’s perspective on the future of programming languages. The idea that we’re moving toward a world where human language IS the programming language and where everyone becomes a programmer feels closer than ever. His point about catching mistakes in prompts before an agent spends 20 min and 💰 executing them really resonated. It’s a simple idea, but it highlights a huge gap in how we currently work with AI.

👉This connects strongly with Alvaro Moya’s talk on Spec-Driven Development—what he framed as the next era of software development. The idea of specs as living contracts between business, teams, and AI models is powerful. A structured way to orchestrate entire fleets of agents while maintaining quality and control? That’s the kind of foundation we’ll need as complexity grows.

And then Cassidy Williams’ keynote tied everything together beautifully 🙌🏼 focusing on how we think and grow in the AI era. A few takeaways I’m still reflecting on:

- Forward-thinking hiring practices. Especially investing in junior talent
- The importance of guardrails when working with AI
- Learning in public now matters more than ever
- If your tools go down, what remains? Your knowledge, your team, your documentation
- And perhaps most importantly: juniors being “mentored” by AI can be both a blessing and a risk, we still need human mentorship more than ever. 

We’re clearly at an inflection point. The tools are evolving fast, but how we design systems, share knowledge, and support each other will define what comes next👌🏻

Huge thanks to Codemotion Chiara Russo Luca Fregoso Michela Bertaina, to the JPMorganChase team in Madrid Miguel Ángel Fajardo Mario Garrido Torres Parley Martins Mike Lewis, to my fantastic MC Dachi Gogotchuri, to my team for the support and to people that came to say hello and ask questions for making this such an unforgettable experience 🤗 #CodemotionMadrid

Build, Buy or Borrow? How to Build the Right Learning Team for 2026

Theatre: Seminar Theatre 1
Date: Wednesday 29th April 2026
Time: 4:15 p.m. - 4:45 p.m.
 
Learning leaders are under pressure to deliver more at a time when the capability mix inside the team is shifting fast. New demands around AI, skills, stakeholder management, platform expertise and business alignment mean that hiring managers are having to make tougher choices about where capability should come from. Should you develop people internally, hire experienced talent into the team, or bring in specialist support on a contract or interim basis? This session will offer a practical framework for thinking through those decisions in a way that reflects the reality of today’s L&D environment.
 
Using the idea of build, buy or borrow as a simple decision model, this session will explore when each approach makes sense, where each can go wrong, and how learning leaders can make smarter decisions about team design, hiring and resourcing. The promise to the audience is straightforward: attendees will leave with a clearer way to assess capability gaps, choose the right resourcing route for the work in front of them, and build a learning team that is fit for purpose rather than simply built around headcount, habit or the loudest immediate pressure.
 
Seminar, Panel Discussion with Q&A:
Wil Procter - Director of Strategy & Innovation at Nazaré
Pinda Dhillon-Sehra - Director of Learning and Development at Centrica plc
Neil Andrew - Talent & Development Expert at Southern Water
Anjali Saraswathyamma - Global Snr. Manager, GTM enablement at Claroty
Mike Ward - Director at Instinct, Learning Recruitment Specialists

Under the EU AI Act, AI systems used in recruitment and employment are classified as high-risk. That adds a different layer of expectations regarding structure, risk identification, ongoing assessment, and documentation that reflects how the system actually works.

And this is where another gap often appears.

Many companies either:
➡️  do not carry out a proper DPIA or treat it as a formal step, disconnected from the product, or
➡️  rely entirely on what the vendor says about the tool

None of these positions is particularly strong if challenged.

“If your internal team is already full and you add an extra project on top… you’re not growing, you’re exhausting them.”

This hit me hard during my conversation with Anna Orzechowska because that's exactly what's happening in most enterprises 🤯

Most companies still think growth = more work for the same people.

And instead of helping them grow faster with independent talent and AI, there is more and more of a "do more with less"...

But in reality? 
That’s not growth. 
That’s burnout 🤷‍♂️

And this is exactly where freelancers come in.
Not as a backup plan, but as a strategic advantage 💪


And here are some key takeaways from our conversation 👇 

🛠 For companies hiring freelancers

1️⃣ Freelancers are not a cost. They are a capability!

You’re not paying for hours. You’re paying for experience that can solve problems faster than your internal team can learn how to solve them.

2️⃣ Stop using freelancers as a last resort.

The best companies don’t wait until things break.
They bring in external experts early to move faster and reduce risk.

3️⃣ Protect your people.

If your team is already at capacity, adding more work is not an ambition. 
It’s mismanagement. Freelancers help you scale without burning out your internal team.


🔥 For freelancers elevating companies:

1️⃣ You are not just a talent. You are a business.

Start thinking in terms of value, outcomes, and positioning. 
Not just hourly rates.

2️⃣ Listen before you act.

The fastest way to lose a client?
Jumping in with solutions before understanding the real problem.

3️⃣ Relationships > transactions.

The best freelancers don’t chase projects.
They build trust, and projects come to them.


We’re moving into a world where the winners won’t be the companies with the biggest teams, but the ones with the smartest mix of internal talent, independent experts, and AI.

🎧 If you want to go deeper into how to actually make this work, check the full conversation with Anna in the comments 👇


P.S. If you’re looking to connect with a top freelancers, freelancermap (international) helps you find strong contract candidates in 48 hours when LinkedIn runs dry 💪

Your last CPO owned cost reduction.

Your next one needs to own resilience, AI adoption, supplier ecosystems, and revenue generation.

Same title. Completely different job.

The CPO role has fundamentally shifted in the last few years. Tariff volatility, nearshoring decisions, AI vendor contracts, Scope 3 compliance, supplier partnerships that open commercial doors, these all sit with procurement now. Whether procurement is ready for them or not.

But most hiring processes haven't caught up. Companies are still screening for category management experience and savings track records. Both matter. Neither is enough anymore.

The result is a hire that looks right on paper and struggles in the role. Not because they're not good. Because the job they were hired to do and the job that actually exists are two different things.

I've seen this play out more than once this year already. The candidate was strong. The brief was outdated. And six months in, everyone's frustrated.
Getting this hire right starts before the search does. It starts with an honest conversation about what the business actually needs from procurement in 2026, not what it needed when the last person was hired.

When did your organization last update what it actually expects from its procurement leadership?

🌟NOW HIRING: Responsabil conformitate și colectare junior (teren)🌟

📣 Pentru clientul nostru, companie activă în domeniul protecției drepturilor de autor, căutăm un Responsabil conformitate și colectare junior (teren). Suntem în căutarea unei persoane aflate la început de drum, cu inițiativă, orientată către dezvoltare și învățare continuă. Mediul de lucru este dinamic, stabil și bine organizat, oferind perspective reale de creștere și dezvoltare profesională.

👉 Rolul postului constă în identificarea în teren a operatorilor economici din zona alocată, încheierea autorizațiilor necesare, colectarea remunerațiilor aferente și gestionarea relației cu utilizatorii, inclusiv urmărirea sumelor restante și informarea acestora privind obligațiile legale.

📍 Locație: Ialomița, Călărași, Ilfov

🔹 Responsabilități principale:
-> Identificarea în teren a operatorilor economici din zona alocată
-> Încheierea autorizațiilor în numele organizației
-> Colectarea remunerațiilor aferente autorizațiilor
-> Urmărirea și solicitarea plăților restante
-> Comunicarea permanentă cu utilizatorii gestionați
-> Informarea acestora privind obligațiile legale aplicabile

Ce oferim:
✔ Contract de muncă full-time
✔ Salariu fix + bonus lunar de performanță în funcție de încasări
✔ Tichete de masă, telefon, tabletă și decont carburant
✔ Mediu de lucru dinamic și oportunități de dezvoltare profesională

💡 Dacă te afli la început de drum și îți dorești un rol cu impact, în care să înveți, să te dezvolți și să ai activitate dinamică de teren, acest rol ar putea fi potrivit pentru tine. 

📥 Dacă acest tip de rol ți-a atras atenția și te consideri potrivit, mi-ar face plăcere să te cunoaștem și să discutăm mai multe detalii. Ca prim pas în procesul de recrutare, te rugăm să ne trimiți un CV detaliat la adresa recrutare1@abchumancapital.ro.

Here's the honest ranking of the 6 GTM models that matter in 2026.

1. Outbound-Led (AI-Enriched).

Pipeline in weeks, not quarters. Waterfall enrichment across 150+ data providers, AI SDRs on first-touch, signal plays on funding and hiring. Cheapest motion to start.
Companies: Clay, Apollo, 11x, Gong, Outreach.

2. Product-Led Growth.

The product is the sales rep. OpenView data shows PLG companies grow 50% vs a 21% SaaS mean. Cracks when enterprise deals need a sales layer.
Companies: Figma, Slack, Notion, Calendly, Canva.

3. Account-Based Marketing.

Markets of one. Intent data from 6sense and Bombora times the plays. 171% ACV lift vs non-ABM accounts. $8K to $30K per month stack.
Companies: 6sense, Demandbase, Bombora, Snowflake.

4. Marketing-Led (Inbound).

Content and SEO pull buyers in. 62% lower CPL than outbound. Compounds for years. But AI overviews are eating organic clicks faster than anyone wants to admit.
Companies: HubSpot, Ahrefs, Semrush, Webflow, Zapier.

5. Sales-Led (Enterprise).

AEs, MEDDIC, 6 to 18 month cycles. Highest contract values. Most predictable math. AE OTE starts at $250K. Wrong motion to pick pre-Series A.
Companies: Salesforce, SAP, ZoomInfo, Clari, Chorus.

6. Community-Led Growth.

Users become the pipeline. Notion runs on 95% organic traffic. Hardest to attribute, deepest moat. Slowest to ROI.
Companies: GitHub, Atlassian, Duolingo, Common Room.

Most founders pick the wrong one for their stage.

The right order in 2026: start with outbound-led, layer PLG when the product can stand alone, add sales-led when ACV clears $50K.

Every other sequence costs runway.

AI didn’t create capacity.
Application and execution did.

If AI is “in use” in your business but not translating into throughput or growth, this is likely why.

Most people arguing about AI and jobs are both right - and both missing half the story.

𝗥𝗼𝗹𝗲𝘀 𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝗲𝘃𝗼𝗹𝘃𝗶𝗻𝗴, 𝗻𝗼𝘁 𝗱𝗶𝘀𝗮𝗽𝗽𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴.

The underwriter who reviewed 40 contracts a week now reviews 200 with AI assist. The job didn't die. It leveled up.

And the static market argument is underrated: demand expands when cost drops. That's not theory - that's what's already happening.

𝗕𝘂𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘁𝗿𝗮𝗻𝘀𝗶𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗼𝗱 𝗶𝘀 𝗯𝗿𝘂𝘁𝗮𝗹.

→ Retraining takes 12–18 months
↳ Upskilling budgets get cut first
→ Entry-level hiring is already slowing in AI-exposed roles

The market isn't static. But 𝑝𝑒𝑜𝑝𝑙𝑒 are - on their own timelines.

The long-run story is optimistic. The medium-run story has real casualties.

If you're leading a team in an AI-exposed function, where are you in that transition window?

The Reality of Staffing in 2026: What Recruiters Must Know

The staffing industry has changed—fast. Today, recruiters aren’t just filling roles, they’re navigating a tech-driven, candidate-first market.
Here’s what’s really happening 👇

1. Stability with Pressure
The market is stabilizing, but expectations are higher—clients want faster, more accurate hiring. Quality beats volume.

2. AI is Changing the Game
AI won’t replace recruiters—but it will replace outdated recruiting habits. Speed + human judgment is the winning combo.

3. Skills Over Degrees
Hiring is now about real skills, not just qualifications. Recruiters must assess capability, not just keywords.

4. Rise of Contract Staffing
Flexibility is key. Contract roles are growing as companies focus on cost control and project-based hiring.

5. Candidate Expectations Are Higher
Speed, transparency, and communication matter more than ever. Recruiters are now brand ambassadors.

6. New Talent Pools
Opportunities are expanding into non-traditional and untapped markets—smart recruiters are exploring them.

Bottom Line:
The industry isn’t shrinking—it’s evolving.

Recruiters who think strategically, use tech smartly, and build strong relationships will stay ahead.

💬 What changes are you seeing in your market?

Most founders think going global means setting up a legal entity first.

That assumption quietly kills expansion timelines — and burns capital before you've made a single hire abroad.

Here's what entity setup actually costs you: 
Legal structuring fees. 
Local registered agents. 
Annual compliance filings.
Local accounting and audit obligations. 
Minimum capital requirements in some jurisdictions. 
And 4–6 months of calendar time, just to get the right to hire.

For a startup validating a new market or an enterprise testing headcount in a region—that's an unreasonable bet to make upfront.

There's a category of infrastructure built exactly for this. It's called Employer of Record (EOR) — and most teams still underuse it.

With Asanify AI's EOR solution, you hire in 50+ countries without touching an entity. 

We become the legal employer on record—handling local contracts, payroll disbursement, tax withholding, statutory benefits, and compliance monitoring — end to end, on Day 1.

What makes Asanify AI different isn't just the geography coverage. It's the AI layer underneath:
— Contracts auto-generated to local labor law standards 
— Payroll processed with AI-led compliance checks, not manual review 
— Real-time alerts when statutory obligations shift in your target country 
— A single dashboard for a globally distributed workforce

If you're a CHRO rethinking global hiring strategy, a founder about to test a new market, or an expansion lead weighing "entity vs. EOR" — the math has changed.

The old model: spend ₹30–80L, wait 6 months, then hire. 
The Asanify AI model: hire first. Expand intelligently. Build the entity only when the market earns it.

Global growth shouldn't be gated by legal infrastructure.

👉 Explore Asanify AI EOR services in the comments.

The U.S. staffing market isn’t slowing down; it’s evolving.

In 2026, companies are still hiring, but with tighter filters, longer approvals, and higher expectations. At the same time, candidates are more selective, prioritizing stability over speed.

What’s changing:
Hiring is now skill-specific, not volume-driven
More interview rounds and slower decisions
Contract roles face higher scrutiny from candidates
AI supports hiring, but human judgment still leads

Reality check: More applicants do not mean faster hiring. Conversion rates are tighter than ever.

What works now: Clear role alignment, transparent communication, and quality over quantity in hiring pipelines.

The takeaway: This is not a slowdown, it’s a recalibration. The teams that adapt will outperform.

How is your hiring strategy evolving in 2026?

l have been working on an AI agent for SEO/GEO - one that can create content, learn from your judgement over time about brand, and keep improving the level of output. Something like OpenClaw for SEO/GEO. 

Reddit Marketing is also going on and going well but given the pushback by Reddit on using technology on the platform - it is hard to scale without humans. Which makes sense given it's the most "human place on the internet". And I do not want to fight the platform on trying to automate it because of the risk. One update and everything can go to zero. So, it's better to do it organically.  I will be lying if I said I did not try to do it. But, every week I had to find new solutions and it's pointless.

The tricky part is also the clients. Hardly 10% of the clients I speak with agree to do it organically. Most do not even believe me when I say I do it organically as a human sitting and engaging. But, it was 0% when I started 8-9 months back so no complaints. It took some time to find people who were interested in doing Reddit organically. A lot of people just trusted me so thank you so much. Grateful to all of them. 

Everyone thinks Reddit is full of bots. It is, but nobody actually cares about them - neither the users nor the LLMs. Also, Reddit discontinued their API very recently in November 2025 (due to obvious reasons, bots) so bots have largely decreased now. 

Going forward, I am not sure if I want to scale humans on the Reddit marketing side. Services business is fun but I don't want something >80% service led. I definitely want to move up the ladder with bigger clients & larger contracts so I can just keep doing this (without hiring & making more money) while also reinvesting money into building some stuff. Somehow merge both the things I do - Reddit and GEO atleast to maybe 70-80% automation if not 100%. Let's see.

Nonetheless, if you or someone you know is looking for Reddit Marketing, please do reach out :)

Under the EU AI Act, AI systems used in recruitment and employment are classified as high-risk. That adds a different layer of expectations regarding structure, risk identification, ongoing assessment, and documentation that reflects how the system actually works.

And this is where another gap often appears.

Many companies either:
➡️  do not carry out a proper DPIA or treat it as a formal step, disconnected from the product, or
➡️  rely entirely on what the vendor says about the tool

None of these positions is particularly strong if challenged.

🌍 Location: Remote (India-based candidates preferred)
⏰ Working Hours: Mon–Fri, 9:00 AM – 5:30 PM CST

Architect and build end-to-end AI/ML systems (data → model → deployment)
Develop full stack applications (React/Vue/Angular + Python backend)
Design and deploy LLM & RAG pipelines, embeddings, and vector DB integrations
Work on AWS + Databricks cloud infrastructure
Implement CI/CD, MLOps, and scalable microservices
Collaborate across teams and contribute to architectural decisions

5+ years in full stack development

Experience building AI pipelines / ML systems in production

Kafka / event-driven systems
